name: AI Now Institute
description: NYU-affiliated research institute, ex-Whittaker. Critical-AI / power-concentration framing: emphasizes labor displacement, surveillance, and Big Tech accountability over catastrophic risk. Influences progressive Dem and EU regulatory thinking.

name: AI-Safety-Aligned Democrats
description: Senators and members emphasizing catastrophic / dangerous-capability framings, pre-deployment evals, and a federal floor on AI rules. Often supportive of NIST AISI funding and voluntary lab safety commitments as a starting point. Loose alignment, not a formal caucus.

name: Anthropic
description: Frontier AI lab developing the Claude family of models. Distinctive policy posture is safety-forward — pioneered Responsible Scaling Policies, publishes interpretability research, and engages substantively with US AISI and UK AISI on pre-deployment evals. Often the lab cited approvingly by AI-safety-aligned legislators.

name: Apple
description: Late and idiosyncratic AI entrant via Apple Intelligence and on-device models. Distinctive policy stance is privacy-forward (Private Cloud Compute) and EU-adversarial (DMA gatekeeper designation, AI feature withholding in EU). Important counterweight to cloud-centric AI policy framings.

name: Bureau of Industry and Security
description: Commerce sub-agency implementing AI/semiconductor export controls. Authors the October 2022 / October 2023 chip rules, the AI Diffusion Rule (tiered country caps), and Entity List designations of Chinese AI firms. The single most consequential US executive actor on AI competitiveness vs. China.

name: California Attorney General
description: Most powerful sub-federal AI enforcer. Bonta's office issued 2025 AI advisories under existing California consumer-protection and civil-rights statutes — effectively a de-facto state AI regime via enforcement, independent of any new AI bill.

name: California Legislature
description: Most prolific state AI lawmaking body. SB-1047 (Wiener) frontier-safety bill, vetoed in 2024, set the template for state-level catastrophic-risk regulation. Continues to push AB-2013 training-data transparency, deepfake/election laws, and labor-AI bills, making CA the de-facto US floor on AI rules.

name: Center for AI Safety
description: Hendrycks-led research and advocacy nonprofit. Authored the 2023 "extinction risk" statement signed by lab leaders; technical advisor to SB-1047. Most credentialed voice for catastrophic-risk framings and dangerous-capability evaluation requirements.

name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
description: Georgetown-housed research center; the most-cited AI policy shop in DC. Runs the primary public datasets on AI talent flows, chip controls effectiveness, and US/China AI competition. Their analysis explicitly shapes BIS export-control rulemaking and Hill briefings on compute thresholds and foreign-talent policy.
aliases: Center for Security and Emerging Technology

name: China MIIT / CAC
description: Combined entry for China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and Cyberspace Administration of China. CAC runs the generative-AI registration and content-control regime (effective Aug 2023); MIIT handles industrial policy, model standards, and chip supply. Together they define the principal foreign AI regime US policy responds to.

name: Coefficient Giving
description: AI-safety and longtermism-focused grantmaker (the recent rebrand of the org formerly known as Longview Philanthropy / EA-aligned funding stream). Funds AI policy fellowships, technical AI safety research, and dangerous-capability evals. Grant flow signals which AI-safety policy theories of change are gaining institutional support.

name: DOJ Antitrust Division
description: Investigates AI-market concentration with FTC under a jurisdictional split (DOJ took Nvidia, Microsoft; FTC took OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). Live merger reviews shape compute-market structure; Google search remedies trial sets precedent for AI-product distribution mandates.

name: Electronic Frontier Foundation
description: Civil-liberties advocacy group. On AI: skeptical of both surveillance-AI and overbroad regulation that would burden open-source. Pro-fair-use in training-data debates; opposed many provisions of state deepfake laws on First Amendment grounds. Orthogonal to safety/x-risk camp.

name: European Commission
description: Executive arm of the EU and operator of the AI Act, the world's first comprehensive AI law. DG CNECT runs implementation; the AI Office handles general-purpose AI rules and enforces obligations on frontier model providers. Brussels effect makes EU rulemaking a de-facto global compliance baseline.

name: Federal Trade Commission
description: Consumer-protection and competition regulator. Under Khan and successors, opened the AI partnerships 6(b) study, pursued Operation AI Comply against deceptive AI claims, and signaled willingness to treat training-data and unfair-method-of-competition theories aggressively. Post-2025 leadership shift partially reorients posture.

name: France / Macron Government
description: Macron's government is the EU's loudest pro-frontier-lab voice — softened EU AI Act foundation-model provisions in 2023 trilogue to protect Mistral. Hosted the Feb 2025 AI Action Summit pivoting from Bletchley's safety frame to a "AI for growth" frame. Key swing actor in EU AI politics.

name: Future of Life Institute
description: Tegmark-led nonprofit. Authored the 2023 "pause giant AI experiments" letter; primary financial backer of safety-pilled policy advocacy in US and EU. Pushes for compute thresholds, licensing, and binding international agreements on frontier AI.

name: Google DeepMind
description: Alphabet's consolidated AI research and product arm (post-2023 merger of Google Brain and DeepMind). Operates Gemini models. Policy footprint spans antitrust (DOJ search remedies, ad-tech), EU AI Act compliance, and copyright litigation. Demis Hassabis is a frequent voice on frontier risk and international governance.

name: Heritage Foundation
description: Conservative think tank, author of Project 2025. AI sections call for dismantling Biden-era AI executive order, restricting "ideologically biased" AI, and aggressive China decoupling. The most direct intellectual feeder to MAGA tech-skeptic policy positions.

name: House Energy and Commerce Committee
description: Primary House committee for AI legislation touching consumer protection, telecom, data privacy, and FTC oversight. Hosts the Innovation, Data, and Commerce subcommittee where federal preemption fights (APRA, AI provisions) play out.

name: Japan METI
description: Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry. Architect of Japan's soft-law, innovation-forward AI approach (AI Guidelines for Business) and the Hiroshima AI Process G7 framework. Operates Japan AI Safety Institute. The principal Asian counterweight to both EU rulemaking and Chinese statism.
aliases: Ministry of Economy Trade and Industry

name: Libertarian / Chamber-Aligned Republicans
description: Republicans favoring permissionless innovation, federal preemption of state AI laws, and minimal ex-ante regulation. Aligned with AEI / R Street / Chamber of Commerce; distinct from MAGA tech-skeptic faction in being pro-industry rather than anti-Big-Tech. Loose alignment, not a formal caucus.

name: MAGA Tech-Skeptic Faction
description: Republican legislators hostile to Big Tech "censorship" and friendly to industrial-policy / China-hawk framings. Skeptical of NIST AISI as a vehicle for "woke AI." Often aligned with Heritage / Project 2025 priorities. Loose, not a formal caucus.

name: MITRE Corporation
description: Federally Funded Research and Development Corporation that runs much of the day-to-day AI standards / red-teaming / evaluation work for NIST AISI, DoD CDAO, and the Intelligence Community. ATLAS adversarial-AI knowledge base, AISI evaluation contracts, and DoD AI test-and-evaluation playbooks all originate here. Quasi-governmental in practice.

name: Meta
description: Operator of the Llama open-weights model family — the central case study in open-source AI policy debates. Yann LeCun is the loudest industry voice against catastrophic-risk framings. Faces concurrent antitrust (FTC Instagram/WhatsApp), Section 230, and EU DMA pressure that bleeds into AI questions.

name: Microsoft
description: Largest enterprise distributor of frontier AI via Azure OpenAI and Copilot. Brad Smith leads visible policy presence (KOSA, content provenance, EU AI Act). Subject to FTC and EU scrutiny over the OpenAI investment and Inflection acqui-hire as quasi-mergers.

name: Mistral AI
description: European frontier AI lab and the loudest commercial voice in EU AI Act trilogue debates. The lead industrial counterweight to US frontier labs in EU policy fights, and the cleanest case study for open-weights frontier model release policy (Mistral 7B, Mixtral).

name: NIST US AI Safety Institute
description: Standards body inside NIST, the operational counterpart to UK AISI. Runs the AI RMF, executes pre-deployment evals under voluntary lab agreements (OpenAI, Anthropic), and is the primary US technical authority on dangerous-capability assessment. Funding and authority are politically contested.

name: New York Attorney General
description: Letitia James's office uses Executive Law §63(12) and Martin Act authority for AI consumer-protection cases (chatbot-deception, hiring-algorithm bias). Pairs with NY Department of Financial Services circulars on AI in insurance underwriting.

name: New York City
description: NYC Local Law 144 (automated employment decision tools) is the only operational US municipal AI bias-audit regime. Enforcement is light but the disclosure model influences state and federal hiring-AI proposals. NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection runs implementation.

name: Nvidia
description: Dominant supplier of AI training and inference GPUs. The pivotal entity for export-control policy: BIS rules on H100/H200/B200 sales to China, and the AI Diffusion Rule's tiered-country framework, are largely Nvidia-shaped. Antitrust scrutiny opening on CUDA lock-in.

name: OECD AI Policy Observatory
description: OECD's AI policy hub. Authored the 2019 OECD AI Principles (revised 2024) — the most-adopted multilateral AI framework, providing the definitional backbone for EU AI Act, US EO, and G7 work. Lower bindingness than EU/US rules but high agenda-setting weight.

name: Office of Science and Technology Policy
description: EOP office that authored the AI Bill of Rights blueprint and coordinates implementation of the AI Executive Order. Lower bindingness than agency rules but sets the cross-agency tone; staffing turnover between administrations is a leading indicator of policy direction.

name: Open Philanthropy
description: The largest funder of AI safety + AI governance research globally. Grant decisions (to RAND AI policy, Georgetown CSET, GovAI, AISI-adjacent technical work, US AI policy fellows) move the AI-safety policy ecosystem; treat funded org outputs as proxies for OpenPhil's substantive priorities.

name: OpenAI
description: Frontier AI lab and ChatGPT operator. Most-watched policy actor: Altman testified for federal licensing in 2023, the lab has shaped voluntary White House safety commitments, and is repeatedly named in copyright suits (NYT, Authors Guild) that will set training-data precedent. Dual nonprofit/PBC structure invites antitrust and governance scrutiny.

name: Populist Democrats (Warren-aligned)
description: Democrats focused on Big Tech antitrust, labor displacement, and concentrated AI-market power. Skeptical of frontier labs as policy partners; favor structural remedies (antitrust, public AI options) over voluntary commitments. Loose alignment, not a formal caucus.

name: Rep. Ro Khanna
description: Representative (D-CA-17), Silicon Valley district. Idiosyncratic profile: progressive on labor and antitrust but pro-innovation and skeptical of heavy-handed AI safety regulation. Bridge figure between populist Dem and tech-industry constituencies.

name: Sen. Ben Ray Luján
description: Senator (D-NM), active on AI workforce, deepfakes, and rural broadband. Co-sponsor of NO FAKES Act and content-provenance bills. Operationally focused on labor and consumer-harm framings rather than catastrophic risk.

name: Sen. Chuck Schumer
description: Senate Majority/Minority Leader (D-NY). Architect of the 2023–2024 SAFE Innovation Framework and the Senate AI Insight Forums. Roadmap was process-heavy and produced no binding bill, making him an indicator more of agenda-setting than enactment. Pro-innovation, skeptical of preemptive heavy regulation.

name: Sen. Maria Cantwell
description: Senator (D-WA), Commerce Committee chair (in D majority). Jurisdiction over NIST/AISI and Commerce AI authorities; shapes any AI legislation moving through Commerce. Pragmatic, industry-engaged given Microsoft/Amazon home-state base.

name: Sen. Ted Cruz
description: Senator (R-TX), Commerce Committee ranking member. Vocal opponent of NIST AISI funding and what he calls "AI censorship"; favors federal preemption of state AI laws. Distinctive voice for the deregulatory-right pole of the AI debate.

name: Sen. Todd Young
description: Senator (R-IN), AI Insight Forum co-lead with Schumer. Author of CHIPS Act framework; views AI primarily through industrial-policy and China-competition lenses. The most reliable Republican partner on bipartisan AI bills.

name: Senate AI Caucus
description: Bipartisan caucus (Heinrich, Rounds co-chairs) functioning as the Senate's AI policy salon. Hosted briefings paralleling the Schumer Insight Forums; lower legislative output than visibility, but a useful venue for tracking which senators show up on AI issues.

name: Supreme Court of the United States
description: Apex court. Loper Bright (2024) overturning Chevron is the load-bearing AI-policy ruling: agency AI rules now face hard-look judicial review on statutory authority. Section 230, content-moderation (Murthy/NetChoice), and forthcoming AI copyright cases route here.

name: TSMC
description: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company — the world's leading contract chip foundry and the single most important non-US company in AI policy. The fulcrum of every chip export-control story (US BIS rules, Taiwan's own export licensing of advanced nodes), and the chokepoint for frontier-AI training compute via Nvidia/AMD GPU production.

name: Texas Attorney General
description: Paxton's office is the right-wing AI enforcement counterweight to CA/NY: launched 2024 inquiry into AI healthcare-platform Pieces, and uses TX consumer-protection statutes against perceived ideological bias and content moderation. Texas TRAIGA bill (2025) signals state-level GOP AI regime.

name: U.S. Congress (general)
description: Catch-all for AI-related bills, resolutions, and floor activity in either chamber of the U.S. Congress not pinned to a specifically-tracked legislator. Backed by the congress.gov adapter scoping to AI-keyword-tagged bills regardless of sponsor; lets the system surface AI legislative activity from members outside the named-individuals roster.

name: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit
description: Appellate court with exclusive jurisdiction over patent appeals; the binding voice on AI inventorship (Thaler v. Vidal) and AI-related patent eligibility. Sets nationwide doctrine for whether AI systems can be inventors and how AI-assisted inventions are claimed.

name: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California
description: Trial-court venue for most major AI copyright and trade-secret cases (Authors Guild v. OpenAI, Kadrey v. Meta, Doe v. GitHub Copilot). Early summary-judgment and class-cert rulings here will shape training-data fair-use doctrine before any appellate decision.

name: UK AI Safety Institute
description: Government-funded technical body, originated at Bletchley Summit 2023. Pre-deployment evals on frontier models under voluntary lab agreements; tightest counterpart to NIST AISI. UK's distinctive non-statutory "context-specific" AI approach gives AISI outsized policy weight.

name: US Patent and Trademark Office
description: Sets policy on AI-assisted invention (Thaler/DABUS aftermath) and AI-generated work copyrightability (jointly with Copyright Office). Issued 2024 inventorship guidance requiring significant human contribution. Decisions ripple into training-data fair-use and authorship debates.

name: xAI
description: Elon Musk's frontier lab, operator of Grok and the Memphis "Colossus" cluster. Politically distinctive: aligned with MAGA tech-skeptic and accelerationist factions, vocal critic of OpenAI governance, and a focal point for content-moderation and election-integrity disputes via X integration.
